data.title: Nvidia Stock Rises. Why the Chip Maker Is Getting a Much-Needed Boost. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By George Glover

Nvidia stock looked set to climb again on Thursday, having racked up gains the previous session thanks to Google parent Alphabet's artificial intelligence spending plans.

Shares in the chip maker rose 0.7% to $125.93 in premarket trading. Futures tracking the benchmark S&P 500 were up 0.2%.

Nvidia stock climbed 5.2% on Wednesday after Alphabet pledged to spend $75 billion on AI this year.

It was a much-needed boost, given that the stock has started 2025 in the red amid fears that the rapid rise of Chinese startup DeepSeek's cheaper AI model could lead to big technology companies rethinking their capital expenditure plans.

Among other chip makers, shares in both Qualcomm and Arm Holdings were sliding on Thursday, even though both companies reported better-than-expected earnings after Wednesday's close.

Write to George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Airthings ASA - Exercise of share options
data.description:
On February 6, 2025, employees in Airthings ASA ("the Company") have exercised1543 400 options. The options had an average strike price of NOK 0.56. Eachshareoption corresponds to one share.

Following the share options exercise, pursuant to authorization granted by theCompany's annual general meeting, the board of directors has resolved toincrease the Company's share capital by NOK 15 434,00 through the issuance of1543 400 new shares, of par value NOK 0.01. Following this increase, theCompany's share capital is NOK 1 993 018,46 divided into 199 301 846 sharesoutstanding, each with a nominal value of NOK 0.01. In utilizing itsauthorization, the board of directors has resolved to set aside existingshareholders' pre-emptive rights to subscribe the new shares in order tosatisfythe Company's obligations under its option program.

No primary insiders exercised options in this round.

For more information, please contact:

Helge Øien, CFO+47 913 19 242helge.oien@airthings.com

https://newsweb.oslobors.no/message/637860

data.title: Honeywell International Preparing to Split Into Three Independent Companies, The Wall Street Journal Reports
CapitaLand Integrated Commercial Trust Has Robust Financials to Upgrade Assets, S&P Says
data.description:
CapitaLand Integrated Commercial Trust is well-positioned to improve its properties and secure its future performance, S&P Global Ratings said in a Thursday release.

The Singapore-based real estate investment trust (REIT) owns high-quality portfolios in prime locations, with upgrades to IMM Building in Singapore and Gallileo in Frankfurt, Germany expected to be completed in H2 of 2025, S&P said.

The REIT may pursue further improvements, especially for properties in weaker markets like North Sydney, according to the rating agency.

Still, S&P expects the REIT to manage its credit profile carefully as it undertakes these projects.

The REIT's funds from operations (FFO) to debt ratio may rise to between 7.6% and 8% in 2025, S&P said.

It may also take on about SG$660 million in additional debt before hitting its target leverage limit of 40%, the rating agency said.

S&P also expects the trust's revenue to expand by 3% to 8% per year until 2026, mainly due to the contributions from ION Orchard and Gallileo.

News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Thursday at 5 AM ET
data.description:
S&P 500 Turns Higher; Google, AMD in Focus

U.S. stocks closed with gains as earnings and economic data retook center stage in markets and political turbulence took a step back.

German Factory Orders Get Late-2024 Boost as Tariffs Loom

Manufacturing orders in Germany rose in December on aerospace demand, although any tentative signs of a recovery for the struggling industrial sector face the imminent threat of tariffs from the U.S.

Hawkish BOJ Board Member Calls for Potentially Faster Rate Hikes

In a speech to business leaders, Naoki Tamura said that the central bank should raise rates to 1% or higher in the fiscal half starting in October.

BOE Expected to Cut Interest Rates. Why That's Good for the Dollar.

Investors are pricing in a 97% chance that the U.K. central bank lowers its key rate to 4.5% from 4.75% on Thursday.

Private Equity Is Booming. Does It Live Up to the Hype?

The $15 trillion market for private assets is thriving. What to know if you want to dip in.

Trump and Musk's Actions Amount to Significant Pullback on Financial Regulation

Agencies overseeing consumer financial affairs, wealth managers, and commodity traders all seem to be scaling back oversight activity.

Fannie, Freddie Privatization, Cost-Cutting and New Name Are Priorities for HUD Chief

Scott Turner says he plans to quickly launch a review to root out inefficiencies at the agency.

Why Trump Is Closing a Trade Exemption for China

Here's how to understand de minimis and how it turbocharged bargain platforms Shein and Temu.

How DeepSeek could deliver another blow to the stock market

DeepSeek's full impact isn't yet reflected in U.S. stock prices, says Don Townswick at Conning

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Thursday at 5 AM ET
data.description:
BYD Shares Rise Sharply Amid Mass-Market Autonomous-Driving Tech Hopes

The gains came after BYD said it will host a press conference next week about its smart-car strategy.

Itochu Considering Investment in 7-Eleven Owner

Japanese trading house Itochu is considering investing in the owner of 7-Eleven, emerging as the latest player in an international takeover battle over one of the world's biggest convenience-store operators.

AstraZeneca Expects More Sales, Earnings Growth After Results Beat Market Views

The pharmaceuticals major was boosted by high demand for its key drugs that offset a slight sales drop in China.

Anglo American Warns of De Beers Impairment Amid Subdued Diamond Demand

The miner said it would examine De Beers's value in light of sluggish demand for diamonds as it seeks to exit the business.

ING Hurt by Higher Loan Loss Provisions

ING Groep net profit fell, missing market forecasts, after it booked loan loss provisions of EUR299 million compared with EUR86 million for the same period a year earlier.

Tesla sales are diving in Germany and across Europe too

Tesla sales careened lower in Germany in January, as sales of the electric vehicle company headed by Elon Musk tumble across Europe.

OpenAI Set to Make Super Bowl Ad Debut

The TV spot marks the artificial-intelligence company's biggest foray into consumer advertising to date.

Amazon Earnings Are Today. Why It's All About AWS.

Wall Street expects Amazon Web Servicesrevenue of $28.8 billion for the quarter, an increase from last year's $24.2 billion.

Eli Lilly's Results Already Disappointed the Street. More Details Come Thursday.

The company disclosed preliminary results in mid January, but it left out important metrics such as earnings per share for the fourth quarter.

Allstate Expects California Wildfires to Cost $1.1 Billion

In the fourth quarter, catastrophe losses took $315 million out of Allstate's homeowners-insurance business, up from $21 million the year before.

data.title: Eurozone Monthly Retail Sales Down 0.2% in December 2024
data.description:
The euro area's retail sales edged down 0.2% month over month in December 2024 following a revised zero growth recorded in the previous month, according to data from Eurostat published Thursday.

Analysts expected a 0.1% decline for the month.

On a yearly basis, the eurozone's retail sales were 1.9% higher, against the revised 1.6% increase earlier. The reading matched the consensus estimate for the period.

data.title: A retail army is bullying bigger investors waiting to buy a stock-market dip, says Goldman strategist
data.description:
By Barbara Kollmeyer

Corporate buybacks also holding up stocks

An unexpectedly large group of retail investors have been pushing their weight around on Wall Street, keeping at bay a buy-the-dip moment that bigger investors have been salivating for.

That's according to Goldman Sachs' Florida-based tactical strategist, Scott Rubner, who told clients in a note on Wednesday that a "record retail army...is new and was not on my bingo card."

"There is a competition for dip alpha, and civilian investors have been pushing around institutional investors given the rally and inability to break lower in equity spot prices," he said. Dip alpha refers to excess returns that could come from a major stock pullback. Examples of institutional money include mutual funds, hedge funds, banks and pension funds.

The furor around DeepSeek last week triggered a 1.5% drop for the S&P 500 SPX and a 3% drop for the Nasdaq Composite COMP on the Monday that news broke, but data shows retail investors bought those pullbacks.

President Donald Trump's tariff announcements at the start of this week initially sparked losses, but indexes ended off their lows and all three are up for the week, and remain positive for the year.

Rubner explained that their data set tracking retail buy imbalances - that is when more investors are buying than selling stocks - showed a record imbalance of $5.03 billion on Jan. 17. That was the biggest since 2019, when they started tracking that and includes the COVID period.

The second biggest retail imbalance was on Monday, of $4.89 billion, with the fifth-biggest in their data set taking place on Tuesday, of $4.23 billion.

So the fact that four of those took place in the last two weeks showed those animal spirits alive and well for retail investors, who jumped at the chance to buy stocks as they fell, even if the drops weren't massive.

Rubner said the stocks that have been seeing the biggest imbalances of buyers versus sellers are also the "most important for index construction" - Nvidia (NVDA) and Tesla (TSLA).

And it's not just retail demand that's providing a tailwind for stocks, but massive corporate buybacks coming into play, Rubner said. "We estimate that 60% of the S&P 500 corporates will have an open repurchase window starting on Friday. This demand dynamic switches on in full force next week," he said.

When companies buy back their own equity, it drives up demand and can push shares higher. And 2025 is shaping up for a blockbuster year, says Rubner, who says Goldman's corporate traders estimate that in 2025, companies on the S&P 500 and Russell 3000 RUA will buy back a record $1.16 trillion worth of stock.

He added that with 250 trading days in 2025, that means $4.64 billion worth of buybacks on average days.

-Barbara Kollmeyer

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Rate-sensitive sectors drag Indian equities lower day ahead of RBI policy
data.description:
India's benchmark indexes closed lower on Thursday, dragged down by rate-sensitive auto and consumer stocks as caution prevailed a day ahead of the Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) rate decision.

The Nifty 50 shed 0.39% to 23,603.35, while the BSE Sensex slipped 0.27% to 78,058.16.

Both benchmarks began the day about 0.3% higher each after logging marginal losses on Wednesday, with sentiment remaining neutral ahead of the RBI's widely expected first interest rate cut in nearly five years.

Nine of the 13 major sectors declined on the day.

Rate-sensitive sectors such as the financials closed flat, auto and consumer firms lost about 1% each and realty stocks shed 2.2%, respectively.

A rate cut, if it happens, will come less than a week after the government, in the federal budget, cut personal tax rates to stimulate consumption.

"Investors will cheer if RBI opts for a rate cut since it will provide a much-needed thrust to economic growth," said Umesh Kumar Mehta, chief investment officer at Samco Mutual Fund.

"But there is rising apprehension over whether a rate cut could potentially hurt the Indian currency, triggering foreign outflows and further piling the pressure on the markets."

The rupee weakened to a record low on the day, weighed down by persistent foreign outflows from domestic capital markets and global trade uncertainty.

Foreign portfolio investors (FPI) have offloaded Indian shares worth $9.23 billion in 2025 so far.

The broader, more domestically focussed smallcaps and midcaps dropped 0.3% and 1.3% respectively.

Food delivery and quick commerce platform Swiggy slid about 7% to a record low after reporting a wider loss in the third quarter.

Winemaker Sula Vineyards dropped 4.2% after posting a 35% slide in its December-quarter profit, hurt by a slowdown in urban consumption.

Electronics distributor Redington climbed 6.4% after its quarterly profit rose, helped by a steady demand for computers and mobile phones.

data.title: Street View: Qualcomm's licensing and Huawei impact to be minimal
data.description:
** Qualcomm on Wednesday forecast sales and profits above analyst expectations as artificial intelligence features helped fuel demand for smartphones, but expected flat revenue from its patent licensing business

** Qualcomm last year stopped selling chips to Huawei but still retained a license agreement with the Chinese telecoms giant

** Shares down 4.2% at $168.50 in premarket trading

WEATHERING THE STORM

** BofA Global Research ("buy", PO: $245) says the results attest to Qualcomm's ability to lead with smartphone technologies and diversify its portfolio into Automotive and Compute

** TD Cowen ("buy", PT: $195) sees the impact of the flat revenue outlook for its patent licensing business and the removal of Huawei royalty to be mild, but says it "adds another brick to the wall of worry facing the stock"

** Barclays ("overweight", PT: $185) says the "ongoing Huawei negotiations and 2 QTL signing delays add some uncertainty to the mix, but otherwise there's not much to pick at here"

** Bernstein ("outperform", PT: $215) says the only minor nitpick might be the Huawei license renegotiations, but impact is small

data.title: Cyprus Inflation Rate Ticks Down to 2.5%
data.description:
Cyprus's annual inflation rate edged down to 2.5% in January 2025, from December’s six-month high of 2.6%.

Price growth slowed for food & non-alcoholic beverages (4.8% vs 7.6% in December 2024), alcoholic beverages & tobacco (2% vs 2.2%), recreation & culture (2.8% vs 2.9%), education (3.7% vs 3.8%), and restaurants & hotels (4.1% vs 4.4%).

At the same time, costs continued to drop for clothing & footwear (-2.7% vs -2.6%) and furnishings, household equipment & supplies (-0.6% vs -0.2%).

In contrast, prices increased for housing & utilities (1.8% vs 1.5%) and transport (3.3% vs 1%).

On a monthly basis, consumer prices decreased by 1.3% in January, following a 0.1% rise in December.

data.title: BAT Malaysia 4Q Net MYR49M
BAT Malaysia 4Q EPS MYR0.1710
JPMorgan says Ethereum likely to keep facing ‘intense competition’ from other networks
data.description:
Ethereum is likely to continue facing "intense competition" from rival blockchains, according to JPMorgan analysts.

While the broader crypto market surged around the U.S. election, Ethereum's native cryptocurrency, ether, has lagged behind — not only underperforming bitcoin but also other altcoins, JPMorgan analysts led by managing director Nikolaos Panigirtzoglou wrote in a report on Wednesday. Ether's share of the total crypto market cap has now dropped to a four-year low, highlighting growing pressure from competitors, the analysts said.

Why is Ethereum underperforming?

The analysts point to two main reasons: rising competition from blockchains such as Solana and Layer 2 networks offering lower fees and greater scalability, and Ethereum's lack of a strong narrative compared to Bitcoin's positioning as a store of value.

Even after Ethereum's Dencun upgrade introduced blobs to reduce fees and improve scalability, activity has increasingly migrated from Ethereum's main network to Layer 2 networks, weakening the base chain, the analysts said.

Rising competition has also driven top decentralized applications to migrate to their own application-specific chains for better performance and lower costs. Uniswap, dYdX, and Hyperliquid have all made this move, with Uniswap's upcoming shift to Unichain being particularly significant, according to the analysts. As one of Ethereum's largest gas consumers, Uniswap's departure could reduce Ethereum's fee revenue and increase the risk of the network becoming inflationary, as fewer transactions would mean lower token burn, the analysts said.

Despite these challenges, Ethereum still leads in stablecoins, DeFi and tokenization areas, though its ability to maintain this dominance remains to be seen, according to the analysts.

To boost institutional adoption, Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin and the Ethereum Foundation recently reportedly invested in Etherealize, a startup founded by former Wall Street trader Vivek Raman. Etherealize focuses on promoting Ethereum to financial firms, highlighting use cases such as tokenization while also developing products to simplify Ethereum's integration with banks.

While tokenization could drive Ethereum's institutional demand, "competition from other networks is likely to remain intense in the foreseeable future," the analysts concluded.

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

data.title: Al Ain Alahlia Insurance to Halt Trading on Abu Dhabi Bourse
data.description:
Al Ain Alahlia Insurance (ADX:ALAIN) shares will temporarily pause trading on the Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange from 11:00 am local time on Feb. 13 due to its board meeting.

The Abu Dhabi-based insurance and reinsurance services provider will resume trading after it discloses the meeting results, according to a Thursday release.

data.title: Cocoa Futures at 3-Week Low
data.description:
Cocoa futures traded around $10,500 per tonne, holding close to their lowest since mid-January, as concerns over demand resurfaced.

Mondelez International, a key player in the chocolate industry, warned of a potential global slowdown in chocolate demand, attributing a larger-than-expected drop in annual profit to "unprecedented" cocoa costs.

At the same time, beneficial rainfall in the key producing region of West Africa has boosted cocoa tree growth and is putting downward pressure on cocoa prices.

Farmers in the Ivory Coast and Ghana report that the recent rains have improved crop conditions and prompted the re-flowering of cocoa trees.

data.title: Japan's Itochu 9-mth net up 11%, firm weighs investing in Seven & i
data.description:
Japanese trading house Itochu posted on Thursday an increase of 11% in nine-month net profit to December of 676.5 billion yen ($4.44 billion) from the corresponding period a year earlier.

The company kept unchanged its full-year profit forecast of 880 billion yen for the fiscal year ending in March.

Itochu is considering whether to invest in Seven & i Holdings , but no decision has yet been made, Chief Financial Officer Tsuyoshi Hachimura told a press conference.

"We have built a track record of firmly implementing investment criteria and only investing in projects that contribute to future growth," Hachimura said.

There was nothing special about the investment in Seven & i, he added, saying Itochu would only invest if stakeholders were satisfied.

Seven & i, the operator of the 7-Eleven convenience store chain, is trying to fend off a $47-billion buyout offer from Canada's Alimentation Couche-Tard (ACT).

After ACT's bid, the Seven & i group's founding family started talks to take the company private for an estimated $58 billion, in what would be the largest management buyout in Japanese history.

Some media have said the family sought cooperation from Itochu, which was considering an investment of 1 trillion yen in the company.

Hachimura suggested there are no funding issues, saying it has improved shareholders' equity over the past years and its credit rating has gone up.

($1=152.2100 yen)

data.title: These Stocks Are Moving the Most Today: Qualcomm, Arm, Skyworks, Ford, Strategy, Amazon, and More — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Joe Woelfel

Stock futures traded higher Thursday as investors looked past worries over tariffs and turned their attention to earnings, particularly from the tech sector.

These stocks were poised to make moves Thursday:

Qualcomm posted fiscal first-quarter adjusted earnings of $2.75 a share, beating Wall Street estimates of $2.46, as revenue jumped 17% to $11.67 billion. Revenue at the company's QCT semiconductor business rose 20% in the period to $10.1 billion. But intellectual property licensing revenue of $1.54 billion missed estimates of $1.56 billion. Qualcomm said it expects fiscal second-quarter revenue of $2.38 to $2.58 a share on revenue of $10.3 billion to $11.2 billion. Shares of the chip maker were falling 4.8% in premarket trading.

Chip designer Arm Holdings reported fiscal third-quarter adjusted earnings that beat analysts' estimates but its guidance for its fourth quarter matched forecasts, disappointing investors and sending the stock down 4%. For the fourth quarter, Arm said it expects adjusted earnings of 52 cents a share on sales of $1.23 billion at the midpoint of its guidance. Wall Street anticipates fourth-quarter earnings of 52 cents a share on sales of $1.22 billion.

Skyworks Solutions plunged 29% after the wireless chip maker said it expected its share of the business to supply Apple, its biggest customer, with radio frequency components would decline by 20% to 25%. The drop, Skyworks said in a conference call, would begin impacting revenue in its fiscal fourth quarter and throughout fiscal 2026. The company also named Philip Brace, who most recently served as CEO of 5G mobile company Inseego, as its next president and CEO.

Ford Motor fell 4.8% after the auto maker posted fourth-quarter operating profit of $2.1 billion on sales of $48.2 billion, better than analysts' estimates on both metrics, but its forecast for operating profit this year of between $7 billion and $8.5 billion missed expectations of about $9.3 billion. Ford also said it expects breakeven adjusted earnings before interest and taxes in the first quarter because of "lower wholesales and unfavorable mix," and expenses related to the launch of some major U.S. assembly plants.

MicroStrategy, the largest corporate holder of Bitcoin, rose 1.3% in premarket trading. The company, which announced Wednesday it now was doing business as Strategy, posted a fourth-quarter loss of $670.8 million, or $3.03 a share. Revenue fell 3% to $120.7 million and missed analysts' estimates of $122.4 million. The company also said it saw its largest-ever increase in quarterly Bitcoin holdings in the period, "culminating in the acquisition of 218,887 bitcoins acquired for $20.5 billion" since the third quarter. The company also said it has so far bought $20 billion of Bitcoin under a three-year plan of buying $42 billion in the cryptocurrency.

Molina Healthcare fell 8.4% after fourth-quarter adjusted earnings of $5.05 a share missed analysts' expectations of $5.88 and the healthcare-services provider said it expected 2025 adjusted earnings of at least $24.50 a share, below analysts' estimates of $25.82.

Meta Platforms rose for a 13th-straight session on Wednesday, the stock's longest winning streak on record. Shares of the parent of Facebook and Instagram also set an intraday high of $718.14, and closed at a record high of $704.87. In premarket trading Thursday, Meta was up 0.4% at $707.85.

Earnings reports are expected Thursday from Amazon.com, Eli Lilly, Honeywell International, ConocoPhillips, Philip Morris International, Neurocrine Biosciences, Bristol Myers Squibb, Fortinet, Air Products, Cloudflare, Roblox, Hershey, and Take-Two Interactive Software.

Amazon.com was up 0.7% in premarket trading. Analysts expect the tech and online retailing giant to report fourth-quarter earnings of $1.49 a share on revenue of $187.3 billion. Wall Street will be watching Amazon's web services business closely after rival tech companies' cloud segments posted disappointing results. Amazon's report is scheduled for after the closing bell Thursday.

Write to Joe Woelfel at joseph.woelfel@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Orsted's Lower Spending Plans Allay Balance Sheet Concerns — Market Talk
data.description: Orsted's cut to capital expenditure combined with its commitment to sell 70 billion-80 billion Danish kroner in assets is likely to bring reassurance on the balance sheet and credit metrics, UBS's Mark Freshney writes. However, a 10% rise in costs on a like-for-like basis is a negative for the Danish renewable-energy company, he adds. UBS says it would appear that the spending cuts relate to delaying or avoiding new projects between 2026 and 2030, so the EBITDA impact is long term. The full-year 2025 EBITDA guidance is very close to the bank's forecasts and only 3% below its forecasts for 2026. "Scaling back the business given cost pressures and no equity raise is a positive surprise." Shares trade 5.9% higher at 297.70 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Update: NCR Atleos Expands ATM Network Into Italy
data.description:
(Updates with the latest stock moves in the last paragraph.)

NCR Atleos said Thursday it has expanded its automatic teller machine network into Italy.

The ATMs will operate under the Cashzone brand in Italy, the 13th country in which NCR Atleos has a presence, the company said.

Shares of the company were up more than 5% in Thursday's premarket activity.

data.title: Australia's Goods Balance Drops in December 2024
data.description:
Australia's trade surplus dropped sharply in December 2024 as imports significantly outweighed exports.

The balance on goods fell to AU$5.09 billion from AU$6.79 in November 2024, according to data published by the Australian Bureau of Statistics on Thursday.

Exports climbed 1.1% from the prior month on the back of the growth of rural goods exports, as well as metal ores and coal. On the other hand, imports soared 5.9% month on month, the highest in fifteen months, as capital goods imports jumped on a monthly basis, the ABS said.

Westpac's senior economist, Mantas Vanagas, said that although Australian exports doubled year on year in the US and grew in China and other countries in Europe during the month, there has been a noticeable drop in exports to New Zealand and other Asian countries.

As for the moment, there is uncertainty on how Australia's trade flow will be affected by escalating trade tensions after US President Donald Trump announced new tariffs against China, Mexico and Canada. There is also the possibility of these tariffs further hiking in China, with the 10% levies being described as an "opening salvo".

"While Australia is unlikely to be among the countries at the top of the President Trump's list for import tariffs, sharp changes in the trade flows in recent months suggest that Australia will not be completely immune to global forces driving international trade," Vanagas said
